title_en: The Major Hadith Collections and How to Use Them
title_ar: الكتب الحديثية الكبرى وكيفية الاستفادة منها
content_en:
Taysir Mustalah al-Hadith concludes with a practical guide to the major hadith collections — their authors, organization, reliability status, and proper use. This section is particularly valuable for students who need to know which collections to consult for different purposes and how to evaluate the authenticity of specific hadiths they encounter.

The Two Sahih Collections

Al-Tahhan begins with the highest-ranked collections: Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim, which together are known as al-Sahihayn (the Two Sahihs). He explains that Sahih al-Bukhari, compiled by Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari (194–256 AH), was the product of sixteen years of careful selection from approximately 600,000 hadiths al-Bukhari had collected. Al-Bukhari reportedly said he included only hadiths he was absolutely confident about, and his criteria were the most stringent of any collector.

Sahih Muslim, compiled by Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj al-Qushayri (206–261 AH), was slightly less stringent in some formal criteria (Muslim sometimes included narrators with minor weaknesses in his supporting chains) but organized more systematically — Muslim gathered all the narrations of a single hadith together in one place, while al-Bukhari sometimes scattered different chains of the same hadith across different chapters. Al-Tahhan notes the classical debate about which collection is superior, presenting the majority view that al-Bukhari's is more rigorously authenticated while Muslim's is more systematically organized.

The Four Sunan

Al-Tahhan covers the four major Sunan collections in order of their scholarly ranking. Sunan Abu Dawud (compiled by Abu Dawud Sulayman ibn al-Ashath, 202–275 AH) contains approximately 5,274 hadiths, many of which specifically pertain to legal rulings, making it the most practically important hadith collection for Islamic law. Sunan al-Tirmidhi (by Muhammad ibn Isa al-Tirmidhi, 209–279 AH) is valuable for its author's practice of noting the reliability of each hadith and the differences between legal schools. Sunan al-Nasa'i (by Ahmad ibn Shu'ayb al-Nasai, 215–303 AH) is considered the most stringent of the four Sunans in its criteria. Sunan Ibn Majah (by Muhammad ibn Yazid Ibn Majah, 209–273 AH) is the weakest of the four in terms of average reliability, containing some hadiths not found elsewhere and a relatively higher proportion of weak narrators.

Other Important Collections

Al-Tahhan also covers the Musnad of Ahmad ibn Hanbal (241 AH), which contains approximately 30,000 hadiths and is the largest of the early collections. Unlike the Sunan which organized hadiths by legal topic, Ahmad organized his Musnad by the name of the Companion from whom each hadith was ultimately transmitted — a different organizational principle that makes it less immediately useful for legal research but valuable as a comprehensive repository of hadith material.

He discusses the Muwatta of Malik ibn Anas as a founding legal-hadith hybrid text, the Sahih of Ibn Khuzaymah and Sahih of Ibn Hibban as later attempts at compilations with strict criteria, and the Mustadrak of al-Hakim as an important but disputed attempt to identify hadiths of sahih quality not included in al-Bukhari and Muslim. The al-Hakim collection's status is controversial because al-Hakim himself was considered relatively lenient in his grading standards, and many of the hadiths he certified as sahih were later assessed differently by al-Dhahabi in his Talkhis al-Mustadrak.

The Enduring Value of Taysir Mustalah al-Hadith

Taysir Mustalah al-Hadith has succeeded in its pedagogical mission: it has introduced thousands of students to the science of hadith methodology in a way that is accessible, systematic, and practically useful. Its combination of clear definitions, worked examples from actual hadith literature, and practical guidance on using the major collections makes it an ideal entry point into a complex and important field. Students who master Taysir Mustalah al-Hadith have the conceptual framework needed to engage seriously with both the classical hadith literature and contemporary scholarship on hadith authentication and use.
